title: SendGrid keys should not be disclosed
description: SendGrid is a cloud-based email delivery platform that provides transactional and marketing email services through SMTP relay and Web API. The SendGrid API uses API keys for authentication, granting programmatic access to send emails, manage email templates, access delivery statistics, configure email settings, manage sender authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), handle suppression lists (bounces, spam reports, unsubscribes), and manage subuser accounts. SendGrid API keys follow a distinctive format starting with 'SG.' followed by two base64-like segments. These keys provide extensive permissions including: sending transactional emails and marketing campaigns, accessing email delivery statistics and engagement metrics (opens, clicks, bounces), managing email templates and dynamic content, configuring sender authentication and domain verification, accessing recipient lists and contact databases, modifying suppression management and unsubscribe groups, managing API key permissions and subuser accounts, accessing webhook event data and email activity, and controlling IP address pools and dedicated IP settings. Exposing SendGrid API keys enables attackers to: send unauthorized emails through your account potentially for spam or phishing campaigns, exhaust email sending quotas causing service disruption, damage sender reputation through spam abuse leading to IP blacklisting, access sensitive email delivery data including recipient lists and engagement metrics, modify email templates to inject malicious content, access bounce and spam complaint data revealing email quality issues, steal customer email lists and contact information, incur financial costs through excessive email sending, compromise sender authentication settings (SPF/DKIM) enabling email spoofing, and access webhook configurations to intercept or modify email event data.
recommendation: Never hardcode SendGrid API keys in source code, configuration files, or version control. Immediately revoke the exposed API key from SendGrid dashboard (Settings → API Keys → Delete), generate a new replacement key with minimal required permissions (use restricted access for specific operations rather than full access), and audit recent API activity for unauthorized email sends or suspicious access patterns. Store API keys securely using environment variables (SENDGRID_API_KEY), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration. Implement least privilege by creating separate API keys with scoped permissions for different use cases (one key for sending emails, another for accessing statistics). Enable IP whitelisting if your infrastructure uses static IP addresses. Rotate API keys regularly (every 90 days recommended) and monitor email sending patterns through SendGrid's activity feed and statistics dashboard. Use SendGrid's subuser feature to isolate permissions for different applications or environments. Review and verify sender authentication settings (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) to ensure they haven't been compromised. Monitor suppression lists for unusual patterns that might indicate abuse.
impact: Critical security risk - Exposed SendGrid API keys grant unauthorized access to email infrastructure enabling spam campaigns, sender reputation damage through IP blacklisting, email quota exhaustion, theft of recipient lists and engagement data, template manipulation, sender authentication compromise, financial costs from excessive sending, service disruption affecting transactional emails, privacy violations from accessing customer email data, and compliance issues from unauthorized email access (GDPR, CAN-SPAM, CASL). Reputation damage and email deliverability issues can have long-term business impact.

compliant.description: Using environment variables for SendGrid credentials
compliant.code:
import os
import sendgrid

# Load SendGrid key from environment
SENDGRID_API_KEY = os.environ.get('SENDGRID_API_KEY')

sg = sendgrid.SendGridAPIClient(api_key=SENDGRID_API_KEY)

checks.message: SendGrid API key detected in source code. This key provides programmatic access to SendGrid's email delivery platform, granting the ability to send transactional and marketing emails, manage templates, access delivery statistics, configure sender authentication, and manage suppression lists. Exposing this API key allows attackers to: send unauthorized emails through your SendGrid account potentially for spam or phishing campaigns damaging your sender reputation and causing IP blacklisting, exhaust your email sending quota (up to millions of emails depending on plan) causing service disruption to legitimate transactional emails like password resets and order confirmations, access sensitive email delivery data including recipient lists, engagement metrics (opens, clicks, bounces), and customer email addresses violating privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA), modify email templates to inject malicious content or phishing links that will be sent to your customers, access and export suppression lists (bounces, spam complaints, unsubscribes) revealing email deliverability issues and customer complaints, steal customer contact databases and recipient lists for competitive intelligence or unauthorized marketing, incur significant financial costs through excessive email sending ($0.30-$3+ per 1000 emails depending on volume and plan), compromise sender authentication settings (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) enabling email spoofing and impersonation attacks, access webhook configurations to intercept email event data (deliveries, opens, clicks, bounces) revealing user behavior patterns, manage subuser accounts and API keys potentially creating additional compromised credentials, modify IP pools and dedicated IP settings affecting overall email deliverability, and access billing information and account settings. For high-volume senders, compromised keys can result in: complete shutdown of mission-critical transactional email flows (authentication, notifications, invoices), immediate IP reputation damage requiring weeks or months to recover, permanent blacklisting by major email providers (Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo), loss of customer trust from phishing emails sent under your brand, compliance violations and fines under CAN-SPAM Act ($43,280 per violation), GDPR fines (up to €20 million or 4% of global revenue), CASL penalties ($10 million CAD per violation), and emergency migration costs to alternative email providers. IMMEDIATELY revoke this API key from SendGrid dashboard at https://app.sendgrid.com/settings/api_keys (Settings → API Keys → [Key Name] → Delete), generate a new replacement API key with minimal required permissions (use 'Restricted Access' for specific scopes like 'Mail Send' only rather than 'Full Access'), audit recent email sending activity in SendGrid Activity Feed for unauthorized sends checking for unusual volumes, recipients, or content patterns, review API usage logs for suspicious access from unexpected IP addresses or geolocations, check email statistics (opens, clicks, bounces, spam reports) for anomalies indicating abuse, verify sender authentication settings (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) haven't been modified, inspect suppression lists for mass additions that might indicate spam campaigns, review webhook configurations to ensure no unauthorized endpoints were added for data exfiltration, check for unauthorized template modifications that could inject malicious content, store API keys securely using environment variables (SENDGRID_API_KEY never hardcoded), secret management systems (AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager), or encrypted configuration (never commit to version control, Docker images, or CI/CD logs), implement least privilege access by creating separate API keys with scoped permissions: 'Mail Send' for sending only, 'Mail Send + Template Engine Read' for templated emails, 'Stats Read' for analytics dashboards, 'Suppression Read' for bounce monitoring, and rotate API keys every 90 days as security best practice, enable IP whitelisting in API key settings if your infrastructure uses static IP addresses to prevent unauthorized access from unknown locations, use SendGrid subusers to isolate permissions for different applications, environments (dev/staging/prod), or teams limiting the blast radius of key compromise, monitor sending patterns through Activity Feed and set up alerts for: sudden spikes in send volume, increased bounce or spam complaint rates, sends to unusual domains or countries, API calls from unexpected IP addresses, and implement rate limiting in applications to prevent abuse even if key is compromised (limit to expected sending volume per hour/day), enable two-factor authentication on SendGrid account to prevent dashboard access compromise, maintain audit logs of all API key creation, modification, and deletion events, use separate API keys for different environments (development, staging, production) to prevent production compromise from test environment exposure, implement email sending policies: sender verification for all from addresses, content filtering to prevent injection attacks, recipient validation to prevent spam, volume throttling to detect abuse, and deploy email security monitoring: DMARC reports analysis, spam complaint monitoring, bounce rate tracking, engagement metric baselines, and regularly review and revoke unused or old API keys to minimize attack surface.
